tokens: Next thing, Jack, you can start verifying your white dots are out.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And you can use your purge valve to depress, if you have to.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, I don't think I have to.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: White dot's plus, for you, EVA decals.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, I'm good here, here, here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That was two EVAs and a half (worth of achievement).
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Thank you, Robert, but until I get my helmet and gloves off, I won't say anything.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And the Heaters (in the) MESA, Open.
You can Open your MESA Heaters (circuit breaker).
We're getting a Master Alarm, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You did get Suit Fan number 2?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You've got Suit Fan number 2 and Delta-P.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's why it (the alarm) came on; (because the fan isn't up to speed).
ECS Caution/Water Sep(arator) component light should go out after that fan comes up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, I never thought I'd wear my EV cover gloves through two EVAs.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, I forgot all about them.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I thought about taking them off until I started chipping those boulders.
And I'm glad I wore them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, I think it's a good idea.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: As hard as it is on your hands, these cover gloves are just ripped to a nub.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (You) might consider taking them off tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You're talking about your cover gloves?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And, I swore (before the flight that) I'd take them off after the drill, but I used a bit of real-time common sense.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think you just about got it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, you went the other way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Did I go the wrong way?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, you get that one, and I'll get these two.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me try this one, now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's usually easier when you do it yourself.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (The wrist ring is) starting to get a little stiff.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Relieved to have the gloves off) Oh, ho, ho, ho.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) Here; I'll get yours for you.
Turn my way, if you can.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't know how they're so wet.
I don't know whether it's...They're just soaking wet.
(Pause) Everything is just twice as hard.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Now comes the old hay fever, again.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That one (visor) up and that one down.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You sure can pick that up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Now, (chuckles) helmets and gloves are off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Read you loud and clear, Challenger.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: The helmets and gloves are off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Why don't you go home and get some sleep, Bob?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I can't say more than that.
And I mean it from the bottom of my heart (pause) or the bottom of my soul or something, my conscience.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, it's all your good training and help...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: As mission scientist, you're totally responsible.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And the backup crew (Young and Duke) says that you are even better than outstanding.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Hey, I'll turn you over to Little Joe (Allen), here...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: ...while I go talk to some people.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We've got a 9 and 1/2 hour EVA scheduled for you tomorrow.
We're planning to spend 2 and 1/2 hours extra over there at Station 4 (at Shorty Crater).
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Laughing) I hope those gloves that you've got packed in the back have got something in them (that is, a cold beer).
See if we can go to bed on time tonight.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I feel better than I did last night, as a matter of fact.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm going to turn you over to Joe.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: That (light) didn't do very much good.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (To himself) Yeah, I verified them both.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (To Bob) Sorry to be touchy, occasionally.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Then you can take your purge valve off.
(Pause) There's a little dust in that (purge valve fitting) tonight.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, let me disconnect yours; you disconnect mine.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think I can get...What did you say?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) Maneuver my fingers in here a little bit better.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: They are all showing a little bit of stickiness.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Glad we're in this (means "doing this") in pairs.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Laughing) Well, just everything's, you know, harder to...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: All right...We don't have the LM hoses on.
So don't...Put mine to Suit Flow to get some air in here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: PLSS pump, Off; and fan, Off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm sorry that's so complex, there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But it was easier to start.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (The dust) smells like someone's been firing a carbine in here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Why don't you stay there and push in that...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Joe, we're reading you loud and clear.
We're on the left-hand column (of the post-EVA card) and we're both going PLSS mode to O, and we'll be off the air for a skosh.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I've been following you real close, and you two are mighty smooth.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (To Gene) Feel how hot that is.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Hey, Challenger has been holding at about 5.5 (psi) ever since we got in here.
Are you all happy with that?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We have been watching it and everyone is happy down here.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, this morning, when we were getting ready, we saw it at 5.5 and part of that (Gene's concern) is that it's been at 5.0 all of the time.
Just so we are not venting anything (into the cabin), that's all.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Geno, we hear you on that and we'll be watching it.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Joe, we're about 2 minutes into the CDR's (PLSS) O2 charge.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Joe, LMP has 96 percent on his (oxygen) gauge.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Geno, with regard to your observation made to us a few moments ago, I guess we will ask for the Cabin Return (Valve) to the Auto position and your
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Joe, CDR is reading 94 percent on the O2 charge.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Say, Joe, our cabin pressure is rising even higher, now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Joe, we had the commander's hoses stowed, but in Suit Flow.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'll look at it a little more here.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Requesting you move your Demand Reg's A and B to Egress, please.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We got the commander's PLSS back in the recharge station.
We got a new battery in it - odd numbers - and a new (LiOH) canister in it and we are working on Jack's right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Do you want me to check the...(Hearing Allen) Okay.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Standby on the battery management for a few minutes, please.
And, in the meantime, could you check the low pressure PLSS fill valve, Closed, please?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (Responding to Allen's instruction about the Power Amplifier) Okay, it's off and we will put that rock in that sample bag and put in SCB-8.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Jack, we're going to ask, for your next act, that you check for us both PLSS valves, Off, and both OPS valves, Off.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Gene and Jack, you'll be interested to hear that the cabin pres(sure) is dropping down, very slowly now.
So, we think we have a tiny leak in one of the (two) cabin demand regulators, and we'll run a check (to see which one is leaking) after you get squared away there a little better.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We'll be at your beck and call.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Joe, we're going to air out the suits.
We're going to go to Suit Flow on the Commander's
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Joe, I guess you guys are tired of looking at my heart beat.
So, I'm gonna turn the Biomed off as I get out of my suit.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: How do you read me (on LM comm)?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, we're going to get Jack out of his suit.
I'll be monitoring (mostly for comm from Houston).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Long Pause) From the way the two of you worked today, I'd think you could just about turn him upside down and pour him out.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Yeah...If he'd fit through that little hole in the end of his wrist.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Joe, the day they can pour me out of anything, they'll call me "Slim".
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The Clean Room will never be the same again.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Geno, we're going to start to investigate which of your demand regulators is leaking and we're going to ask you to put Demand Reg Alpha to Cabin now.
And, as we watch it, please do not make any
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, we will not make any urine dumps and we'll go to Cabin now.
And we'll be ready for your debriefing here in about 5 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, it's going to be a short one.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And, okay; we are ready for your EVA-2 debriefing.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: To begin with, we want you to delete that step going to Down-voice, Backup.
And I've got a surface block data to read up to you.
A few minor changes in your Lunar Surface Checklist.
And a couple of very quick questions for the debriefing when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Go ahead, in the stated order.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Moving right along now to the surface block data.
Lift-off times: T-33, 152 plus 30 plus 01; T-34, 154 plus 28 plus 33; T-35, 156 plus 27 plus 05; T-36, 158 plus 25 plus 37; T-37, 160 plus 24 plus 09.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Hey, Joe, for pantry purposes, what day is this?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've checked around the room here and the consensus is that it's (3:23 a.m.
Central Standard Time) Wednesday morning (13 December 1972).
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I really wanted to know whether it was irradiated ham or frankfurter morning, and I guess we can work that out.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Apparently, the Surgeon is happy with either of those days.
And we want you to turn, right now, to (page)
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: You don't want me to go on the Verb...
You're not going to give us an update, huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That was just to prevent an overflow.
And then I'm ready for the quick changes in the Lunar Surface Checklist when you are.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Begin by putting the Demand Reg Bravo to Cabin position and leave the Demand Reg Alpha in the Cabin position where it now is.
And I'll continue on with the changes in the checklist here.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I changed the mags in the "Empty" from Kilo to Romeo and the mag in "Stow" from Romeo to Kilo.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, that sounds like the thing to do.
And a note on your demand regulators.
We're showing that the demand regulator Alpha has good integrity and we're now in the process of checking demand regulator Bravo.
I've got a couple of fairly quick questions here when you're ready for those.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Go ahead with your questions; and integrity is certainly what we need around here, right?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, a question for you to begin with.
Is your gold (electroplated) visor sticking halfway down?
Apparently, that's based on a discussion earlier.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: No, he said his sunshade is sticking halfway down, but his gold visor's not.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We couldn't tell from the TV whether it was the visor or the sunshade.
We also heard some discussion about possible wear in the seats of the suits when you were dusting each other off.
We want to know if you could see any hint of the aluminum layers showing through in the suits.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: It's just a few scars on the PLSS thermal blanket in back where you probably rub the seat when you get in.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Now two real quick geology questions that will help us do the planning for your EVA tomorrow.
The first one has to do with Station 4.
And you called out some material on the rim (of) the crater at Station 4 which looked like "bedded spatter".
And we're wondering if that resembled things that you'd seen in Hawaii?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Hey, Joe, I think they misheard.
I think I may have said "shattered" and you might have thought "spattered".
No, I didn't - neither one of us - intended to leave that impression.
The big rock we sampled looked like intensely-shattered gabbro, such as we've had around the LM.
Probably more significantly, the rocks - one of which Gene picked up - (were) the fine-grained, coarsely-vesicular basalts.
And we didn't have any time to really examine the interrelationships of those rock types there, but those were the two fragment types we saw.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's quite clear to us now.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: The bottom of that crater, now, had material that was extremely disorganized in its aspect and, really, we didn't have time to examine it in detail in order to decide why it was disorganized.
It did not necessarily look (in texture) like the boulder that we sampled at the rim.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: A question about the boulder you sampled at the rim.
Would you compare the basalt in this boulder - which you may have called a gabbro, I'm not sure, in any case a basalt - to samples which you collected at Camelot and at ALSEP.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, my impression was that they were the same rock types.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's it for us on the questions.
And for information, we're showing your cabin pressure is holding fairly steady even with both those demand regs on.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Keep watching it for us, would you, and let us know.
I expect one of them is probably leaking pretty slow.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah; no worry about that, Gene.
We're looking at it real close.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: How's (Command Module) America looking to you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It is just as clean as a whistle.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: It may not be when we get back there, judging from the looks of us.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Joe, is there any...You got any more debriefing questions?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And we're interested that you move right along so we can get you turned in there and get some rest.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We're eating now, and we feel the same way, I think.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And at your convenience, you can go ahead with the (PLSS) feedwater recharge.
We want you to hold off on the oxygen recharge until we watch these regs for about another 10 minutes.
And give us a mark if you do start the water recharge, please.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: And if there are any ways we can cut corners on the time here, it'll be helping us, because we're still looking at being down a couple of hours nearly.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We're working at it as fast as we can.
(Pause) Best place in the world to make it up is tomorrow night.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Right, Geno, and, actually, we're going to pick up a good one shortly, because we're coming up to a pad in the timeline.
So, as long as we don't waste too much time, we're doing pretty well.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: There's just a certain amount of housekeeping we have to do.
But, very seriously, day after tomorrow is a very short day, and I think we ought to look at making up any time.
I'm a hold-faster on sleep periods but tomorrow('s rest period) is the one that I think is flexible.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Won't be too long at it, but if you've got any significant news or anything, why don't you give it to us?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, I don't know if it's significant news, but at least I know you'll be interested.
Both your demand regs look good now.
We show no evidence of a leak there and it may have been that just recycling them reseated them and solved whatever problem we had.
You can go ahead with the O2 (top-off) recharge on the PLSS and the water recharge at your convenience.
And let me poll the room here for other news items.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay, Joe, we're starting an O2 charge of the CDR's PLSS; (that is, recharge for) ten minutes.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: This is a news report to eat by.
I'll combine an orbital science report with a sports report, an unusual combination here.
I'll start out with a sports report on Monday Night Football, which you may not have heard yet.
Joe Namath tried mightily to lead the New York Jets into the American Football League playoffs, but the Oakland Raiders grounded the Jets in a fourth quarter 24 to 16 blitz.
Namath passed for more than 400 yards but, in spite of it, New York scored only one touchdown.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Moving along to the successes of Captain America, I'll run down different items in the SIM bay here, beginning with the UV spectrometer.
In general, the data has been excellent.
We're getting indications that the hydrogen atmosphere of the Moon is much less than expected.
In fact, I don't think we're detecting any, but rather setting a limit on the amount of hydrogen around the Moon.

There was an Aerobee launch...(correcting himself) or, an attempt at an Aerobee launch from White Sands on Monday to calibrate solar UV radiation, but this launch failed because an instrument viewing port in the rocket failed to open.
A second launch - let's see - was scheduled, I think, for today, and I don't know whether that was successful or not.
(Pause) I guess it'll be launched later today.
The infrared scanning radiometer is performing beautifully.
Indications are that subsolar-point surface temperatures are higher than we've detected from our Earth-based observations before.
We're seeing many thermal anomalies, particularly in (Mare) Procellarum - in the Procellarum area west of Copernicus.
And we're seeing also a few unusual cold spots, which apparently are indicating areas of very fine soil with a few or no blocks in and on the soil.
The lunar sounder data is beautiful, and the power monitor signals we find correlate with the surface features.
And the HF (high-frequency radio) data indicates to us that we are detecting a variety of layers in the mare areas.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Do you know where specifically they're seeing the hot spots west of Copernicus?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, I don't have it on the page in front of me here.
We're going to check into it and I'll get back to you in a second.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: With regard to your questions on the hot spots, apparently they've not yet indexed these warmer sources that they're detecting to the CSM ephemeris, and so they don't know exactly what they correspond to as far as the surface features themselves go.
So I can't help you on that right now.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Gene and Jack, TELMU handed me some numbers which I think you will be interested in.
From the EVA-2 EMU summary, the elapsed EVA time was 7 hours plus 37 minutes plus 22 seconds for a new outdoor record under international rules.
The rest of the sheet looks free from problems in a comforting way.
Let's see, average metabolic rates: for you, Geno, 855 (BTU per hour); and, Jack, you're running at around 920.
And that's relative to pre-mission (estimated) averages of around 850.
And you have a grand total EVA time now of 14 hours 49 minutes and 35 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Do you have any idea how the metabolic rate compared to yesterday?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me ask on that one.
(Pause) Yesterday you were running at 1045 and 1090.
So you're down considerably from your work rates of yesterday, which is good news.
Maybe you're learning how to do it more easily or something like that.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Yeah, but we spent a lot of time riding today and a lot of time working yesterday.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I guess that's not taken into consideration of the average here.
We can ask for the metabolic rate of the Rover.
I bet that is pretty impressive for today.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Driving that Rover is soft; but, I'll tell you, it keeps your attention.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It keeps the passenger's attention, too!
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We noted some comments when you were rolling along today; and reading between the lines from time to time.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Actually, Joe, for good long spans of the run up to Station 2, except when we had to pick our way up the Hole-in-the-Wall, I was running full bore at anywhere from, I guess, what'd I say, 10 to 12 to 15 clicks.
I didn't hit 15 going up (to Station 2) very much.
Coming down I did, but it's really a "standby for a turn and watch where you're going" type of run.
Because the small craters, of course, are the ones that can really jolt you.

But the trouble is, you can never see what's just over the next ridge, and the next ridge may be 20 meters away and you just can't see it until you're there, and you don't know whether its a dish crater or whether it's a pit crater.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That description fits the geology up in there (on the Scarp), because we weren't seeing blocky rimmed craters and otherwise you would have been able to tell more easily about the old versus new craters, which would be the ones you could either go through or not go through, respectively.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's a super machine to drive though, Joe, I'll tell you.
If you had enough time you could really learn to take it all the way.
But you don't really do that, just the second time around.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Geno, was it spraying dirt at you today?
Could you notice that you still missed the real fender and that the patched fender wasn't quite doing what maybe it could?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: No, sir, I don't think we missed it at all.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Fact is, we're recommending a design change, Joe.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That'll be for next year's models.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Is it all right to use the waste management system (to make a urine dump)?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're happy with those demand regs now.
And you can proceed on with that (urine dump) and including all the PLSS recharges that you'll need to do as well.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Joe, we're filling my PLSS with water now.
You might check on the (change in the) water quantity (in the descent stage tanks during the fill).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Joe, that should take care of my PLSS for tonight.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Out of curiosity, have you packed, or are you packing, the ETB now (for EVA-3)?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay; we've got a last minute change.
We show that your mag Bravo is at about 77 frames, and we'd like for you to leave it in the ETB - it is already in the ETB - and take it out with you tomorrow.
We can shoot up the remaining frames if we run out of film otherwise.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: That goes along with our thinking.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You know, apparently you made some comment earlier in the day about being bothered by comm noise during your egress from Challenger.
Did that go away right away, or did it just cease to bother you?
What was the story on that?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: So, it must have gone away, because the comm was great.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay; that's what we kind of assumed.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay; we're charging Jack's PLSS with oxygen.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The O2 fill is complete on the LMP's PLSS, and we're working on the water.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Challenger, for your information, we're coming up on (a) comm handover in about a minute and a half.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Joe, how's the weather gotten down there?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We were really socked in yesterday.
That front's moved on through the Houston area, and it is cold and clear tonight, I suspect.
It's been a while since we've been out, but they are calling for it to go down right near freezing.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: (Have) they succeeded in leveling the gravimeter (the LSG), yet?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, we'll check it, update our information on that.

My understanding at the moment is that they've not; but they're thinking that the unit's just too cold and they're in the process of warming it up by dumping heat into it by running some of the equipment around it and in it, and they've by no means given up hope for that unit.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I figure that means that my fooling around with it didn't help 'em.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Apparently, it didn't do too much for them, but what it did do was convince them that it's probably somehow locked up because its temperature's not right yet.
And they're not worrying about whether it's level or not level now.
They're confident that it's been set up okay, and now they're just biding their time to bring that temperature up.
We'll get some more words to you sometime tomorrow on it as you make your traditional visit to the ALSEP site again, probably.
How are you coming along with your sleep prep?
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: We picked up some time somewhere in here.
Couldn't be much more than an hour behind.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You're looking pretty good on that.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: If you get to sleep in the next 5 minutes, you're one hour behind.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: What's he (Slayton) doing up so late?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, somebody's got to sit up and keep you guys honest.
(Pause) I think we're getting more sleep down here than you are.
transcript.speaker: Slayton

tokens: I might add that not only do we have to stay up late, we have to get up mighty early to keep you honest, too.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Okay; you going to let us sleep 8 hours or what?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're looking good on the time, and not only will you get, we hope, 8 hours of good sleep, but you'll have a full EVA tomorrow.
So, it's not costing us anything there.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I fully expect it won't be much longer now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, Gene, just for rough planning purposes, we'll start to figure your sleep period starting around 152:30.
And we'll be looking at your getting up around 8 hours from that time.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Might add, also, that there are a lot of us looking forward to that third EVA tomorrow.
It's going to be the last one on the lunar surface for some time.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: I tell you, if it's anywhere near what the first two were like, we're looking forward to it, also.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene and Jack, we're still marveling at the beautiful television pictures that we're getting from your TV camera there.
It's fun, in fact, to watch the tracks that you're leaving behind in the lunar soil, both footprints and Rover tracks.
And some of us are down here now reflecting on what sort of mark or track will - someday - disturb the tracks that you leave behind there tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: That's an interesting thought, Joe, but I think we all know that somewhere, someday, someone will be here to disturb those tracks.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I think it's going to happen.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oh, there's no doubt about that.
But it's fun to think about what sort of device will ultimately disturb your tracks.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Well, that device may look something like your little boy.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ah; he'd make short work of them.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Joe, I'll tell you it's also a pretty philosophical thought to think that you're riding around out here on what is really undisturbed everything, you know.

If there was someone here, way back when sometime, they didn't leave much sign of their whereabouts, but that's an interesting thought, too, as you drive around and all of a sudden cross your own Rover tracks and figure out those are the only ones that have maybe have ever been here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And with that, I'm rolling out my hammock.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm rolling up my overhead (Rendezvous) window cover.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll say good night to you from down here, unless there's some other way we can help you.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: If there is, we'll give you a call, though.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Just want to end by saying what a terrific job you did today, and we're really looking forward to tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Allen

tokens: Tomorrow we answer all the unanswered questions.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt
